is one of nine official [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") at the [first level](/wiki/ITL_1_statistical_regions_of_England "ITL 1 statistical regions of England") of [International Territorial Level](/wiki/International_Territorial_Level "International Territorial Level") for [statistical](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") purposes. It covers the western half of the area known traditionally as the [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(England) "Midlands (England)"). The region consists of the [counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_england "Ceremonial counties of england") of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire"). The region has seven cities: [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford"), [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield"), [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent"), [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton") and [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England").

The **West Midlands** is one of nine official [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") at the [first level](/wiki/ITL_1_statistical_regions_of_England "ITL 1 statistical regions of England") of [International Territorial Level](/wiki/International_Territorial_Level "International Territorial Level") for [statistical](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") purposes. It covers the western half of the area known traditionally as the [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(England) "Midlands (England)"). The region consists of the [counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_england "Ceremonial counties of england") of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire"). The region has seven cities: [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford"), [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield"), [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent"), [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton") and [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England").

The West Midlands region is geographically diverse, from the urban central areas of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation") to the rural counties of Herefordshire, Shropshire which border [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), and Worcestershire. The region is landlocked; however, the longest river in the UK, the [River Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn"), traverses the region south-eastwards, flowing through the [county towns](/wiki/County_town "County town") of [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury") and Worcester, and the [Ironbridge Gorge](/wiki/Ironbridge_Gorge "Ironbridge Gorge"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"). Staffordshire is home to the industrialised [Potteries](/wiki/Staffordshire_Potteries "Staffordshire Potteries") conurbation, including the city of [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") and the [Staffordshire Moorlands](/wiki/Staffordshire_Moorlands "Staffordshire Moorlands") area, which borders the south-eastern [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District_National_Park "Peak District National Park") near [Leek](/wiki/Leek,_Staffordshire "Leek, Staffordshire"). The region also encompasses five [Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Areas_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty"): the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley"), [Shropshire Hills](/wiki/Shropshire_Hills "Shropshire Hills"), [Cannock Chase](/wiki/Cannock_Chase "Cannock Chase"), [Malvern Hills](/wiki/Malvern_Hills "Malvern Hills") and parts of the [Cotswolds](/wiki/Cotswolds "Cotswolds"). Warwickshire is home to the towns of [Stratford upon Avon](/wiki/Stratford_upon_Avon "Stratford upon Avon"), birthplace of writer [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"); [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire"), the birthplace of [Rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"); and [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton"), birthplace to author [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

The official region contains the [ceremonial counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire").

There is some confusion in the use of the term "West Midlands", as the name is also used for the much smaller [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") and [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation") which is in the central belt of the Midlands and on the eastern side of the West Midlands Region. It is also still used by various organisations within that area, such as [West Midlands Police](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police "West Midlands Police") and [West Midlands Fire Service](/wiki/West_Midlands_Fire_Service "West Midlands Fire Service").

The highest point in the region is [Black Mountain](/wiki/Black_Mountain_(hill) "Black Mountain (hill)"), at 703 metres (2,307 ft) in west Herefordshire on the border with [Powys, Wales](/wiki/Powys,_Wales "Powys, Wales").
The region contains five [Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Areas_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty") (AONBs), including the [Shropshire Hills](/wiki/Shropshire_Hills "Shropshire Hills"), [Malvern Hills](/wiki/Malvern_Hills "Malvern Hills") and [Cannock Chase](/wiki/Cannock_Chase "Cannock Chase"), and parts of the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley") and [Cotswolds](/wiki/Cotswolds "Cotswolds"). The [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District_National_Park "Peak District National Park") also stretches into north [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire").

## Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern history")]

Further information: [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia")

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Second World War")]

The [RAF Fauld explosion](/wiki/RAF_Fauld_explosion "RAF Fauld explosion") on 27 November 1944 in east Staffordshire produced a 100-foot deep crater, and is the UK's largest explosion, being caused by around 4,000 tonnes of [high explosive](/wiki/Explosive_material "Explosive material"), and may be the [world's largest non-nuclear explosion](/wiki/Largest_artificial_non-nuclear_explosions "Largest artificial non-nuclear explosions").

Birmingham was the third [most bombed](/wiki/Birmingham_Blitz "Birmingham Blitz") city in the UK after London and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"); Spitfires were built in Castle Bromwich, [Lancasters](/wiki/Avro_Lancaster "Avro Lancaster") at Austin's works in [Longbridge](/wiki/Longbridge_plant "Longbridge plant") at [Cofton Hackett](/wiki/Cofton_Hackett "Cofton Hackett"), and the [Birmingham Small Arms Company](/wiki/Birmingham_Small_Arms_Company "Birmingham Small Arms Company") at [Small Heath](/wiki/Small_Heath,_Birmingham "Small Heath, Birmingham") produced the [M1919 Browning machine gun](/wiki/M1919_Browning_machine_gun "M1919 Browning machine gun"). [Boulton Paul Aircraft](/wiki/Boulton_Paul_Aircraft "Boulton Paul Aircraft") had their main aircraft factory in the north of Wolverhampton. [RAF Defford](/wiki/RAF_Defford "RAF Defford"), in the south of Worcestershire between Pershore and [Croome Park](/wiki/Croome_Court "Croome Court"), was where many important [airborne radars](/wiki/Airborne_early_warning_and_control "Airborne early warning and control") were developed, such as [H2S (radar)](/wiki/H2S_(radar) "H2S (radar)") and anti-submarine radars.

### Scientific heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Scientific heritage")]

[Thomas Wedgwood](/wiki/Thomas_Wedgwood_(photographer) "Thomas Wedgwood (photographer)"), son of Josiah Wedgwood, discovered the first [photo-sensitive](/wiki/Photographic_paper "Photographic paper") (light-sensitive) chemicals – [silver nitrate](/wiki/Silver_nitrate "Silver nitrate") and [silver chloride](/wiki/Silver_chloride "Silver chloride") in the 1790s.

Sir [Norman Lockyer](/wiki/Norman_Lockyer "Norman Lockyer") of [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire") discovered [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") in 1868, for which he used electromagnetic [spectroscopy](/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy").

[Edward Weston](/wiki/Edward_Weston_(chemist) "Edward Weston (chemist)") of Oswestry, who emigrated to the US, built the first accurate [voltmeter](/wiki/Voltmeter "Voltmeter") in the late 1880s, and the [Weston cell](/wiki/Weston_cell "Weston cell") in 1893.

[Francis W. Aston](/wiki/Francis_William_Aston "Francis William Aston") of Harborne, educated at the University of Birmingham, developed [mass spectrometry](/wiki/Mass_spectrometry "Mass spectrometry") in 1919, which helped him to identify the first [isotopes](/wiki/Isotope "Isotope"), receiving the [Nobel Prize for Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") in 1922.

[Dennis Gabor](/wiki/Dennis_Gabor "Dennis Gabor") invented holography at [British Thomson-Houston](/wiki/British_Thomson-Houston "British Thomson-Houston") in Rugby in 1947, receiving the [Nobel Prize for Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") in 1971.

[James Glaisher](/wiki/James_Glaisher "James Glaisher") in 1862 took a record balloon flight with [Henry Tracey Coxwell](/wiki/Henry_Tracey_Coxwell "Henry Tracey Coxwell") for the [BAAS](/wiki/British_Science_Association "British Science Association") near Wolverhampton. They reached 29,000 feet (8,800 m) the composition of the Earth's atmosphere until then was not understood; the altitude records for the UK have not been exceeded since; [Project Excelsior](/wiki/Project_Excelsior "Project Excelsior") in the US in 1960 would later reach 20 miles (110,000 ft).

[Philip Lawley](/wiki/Philip_Lawley "Philip Lawley") of Burton upon Trent was first person to realise that [chemical damage to DNA](/wiki/Health_effects_of_tobacco "Health effects of tobacco") caused cancer (at the Chester Beatty Research Institute in London) in the early 1960s.

[Francis Galton](/wiki/Francis_Galton "Francis Galton") (d. 1911) of the [Darwin–Wedgwood family](/wiki/Darwin%E2%80%93Wedgwood_family "Darwin–Wedgwood family")'s Birmingham branch was an early [eugenicist](/wiki/Eugenics "Eugenics") rooted in improving animal breeding stock and examining heredity. He invented terms eugenics and *[nature versus nurture](/wiki/Nature_versus_nurture "Nature versus nurture")*. His limited calls for human eugenics were widened by the [German Society for Racial Hygiene](/wiki/German_Society_for_Racial_Hygiene "German Society for Racial Hygiene") in 1905 founded by [Alfred Ploetz](/wiki/Alfred_Ploetz "Alfred Ploetz"), which coupled with the racial superiority fallacies of [Aryanism](/wiki/Aryan_race "Aryan race") reached its nadir in [genocidal](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"). Moral teachings and [inherent repulsions](/wiki/Deontological_ethics "Deontological ethics") towards human eugenics were overcome by a minority of those in power espousing [racial equality](/wiki/Racial_equality "Racial equality"); European media and leaders lamented [loss of Empire](/wiki/European_imperialism "European imperialism"), advocated [ultranationalism](/wiki/Ultranationalism "Ultranationalism") and prized military physical advantage; Galton saw human eugenics as part of all means to *do better*.

### Industrial heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Industrial heritage")]

See also: [Science and invention in Birmingham](/wiki/Science_and_invention_in_Birmingham "Science and invention in Birmingham") and [Architecture of Birmingham](/wiki/Architecture_of_Birmingham "Architecture of Birmingham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/The_Iron_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_224230.jpg/250px-The_Iron_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_224230.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Iron_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_224230.jpg)

The [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") Iron Bridge at [Coalbrookdale](/wiki/Coalbrookdale "Coalbrookdale"), opened in January 1781, was the first large-scale object made out of cast iron; but cast iron is not reliably strong due to impurities. [Wrought iron](/wiki/Wrought_iron "Wrought iron"), where the carbon is hammered to remove the carbon and impurities is much stronger; the first large-scale wrought iron bridge was the [Britannia Bridge](/wiki/Britannia_Bridge "Britannia Bridge") over the [Menai Strait](/wiki/Menai_Strait "Menai Strait"), only possible due to its innovative [box girder](/wiki/Box_girder "Box girder") design by [Robert Stephenson](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson "Robert Stephenson").

Much of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") in the United Kingdom began in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country") area of West Midlands. The Industrial Revolution is thought to have begun when [Abraham Darby](/wiki/Abraham_Darby_I "Abraham Darby I") substituted [coke](/wiki/Coke_(fuel) "Coke (fuel)") in the place of charcoal to smelt iron, at his Old Furnace. The Black Country may be regarded as the world's first industrial landscape,[[6]](#cite_note-6) while nearby [Ironbridge Gorge](/wiki/Ironbridge_Gorge "Ironbridge Gorge") claims to be the Birthplace of Industry.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [The world's first cast iron bridge in 1779](/wiki/The_Iron_Bridge "The Iron Bridge") spans the Gorge. The first self-propelled locomotive to run on rails in 1803 at Coalbrookdale, was built by [Richard Trevithick](/wiki/Richard_Trevithick "Richard Trevithick"). The [first iron rails](/wiki/Permanent_way_(history) "Permanent way (history)") for horse-drawn transport, were made at Coalbrookdale in 1768 by [Richard Reynolds](/wiki/Richard_Reynolds_(ironmaster) "Richard Reynolds (ironmaster)") at [Ketley Ironworks](/wiki/Ketley_Ironworks "Ketley Ironworks"). Iron rails only became widely successful in 1820 when made out of [wrought iron](/wiki/Wrought_iron "Wrought iron") at [Bedlington Ironworks](/wiki/Bedlington_Ironworks "Bedlington Ironworks") in north-east England.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Philipp_Jakob_Loutherbourg_d._J._002.jpg/250px-Philipp_Jakob_Loutherbourg_d._J._002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philipp_Jakob_Loutherbourg_d._J._002.jpg)

*Coalbrookdale by Night*, of the [Madeley Wood Company](/wiki/Madeley_Wood_Company "Madeley Wood Company"), painted by [Philip James de Loutherbourg](/wiki/Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg "Philip James de Loutherbourg") in 1801

Birmingham's industrial development was triggered by discussions at the [Lunar Society of Birmingham](/wiki/Lunar_Society_of_Birmingham "Lunar Society of Birmingham") at [Soho House](/wiki/Soho_House "Soho House"), Boulton's house, and products were carried along the [BCN Main Line](/wiki/BCN_Main_Line "BCN Main Line") canal. [Soho Manufactory](/wiki/Soho_Manufactory "Soho Manufactory") was the first man-made-powered factory in world. [Chance Brothers](/wiki/Chance_Brothers "Chance Brothers") of Smethwick built the glass for [The Crystal Palace](/wiki/The_Crystal_Palace "The Crystal Palace") in 1851. [Smethwick Engine](/wiki/Smethwick_Engine "Smethwick Engine"), now at [Thinktank, Birmingham Science Museum](/wiki/Thinktank,_Birmingham_Science_Museum "Thinktank, Birmingham Science Museum"), is the oldest working steam engine, made in 1779, and is the oldest working engine in the world. Smethwick was a main centre for making [lighthouse](/wiki/Lighthouse "Lighthouse") [lanterns](/wiki/Lantern "Lantern").

[Valor Fires](/wiki/Yale_%26_Valor "Yale & Valor") in Erdington developed the first [radiant](/wiki/Radiant_heating "Radiant heating") [gas fire](/wiki/Gas_heater "Gas heater") in 1967, a balanced [flue](/wiki/Flue "Flue") fire in 1973, and a natural flame gas fire in 1978. The Erdington site, owned by Iceland's [BDR Thermea](/wiki/BDR_Thermea "BDR Thermea"), closed in May 2012. The company also built [gas cookers](/wiki/Gas_stove "Gas stove"); since 2011 the company has been part of [Glen Dimplex](/wiki/Glen_Dimplex "Glen Dimplex"), who have a site at [Cooper's Bank](/w/index.php?title=Cooper%27s_Bank&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cooper's Bank (page does not exist)"), south of [Gornalwood](/wiki/Gornal,_West_Midlands "Gornal, West Midlands").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Boulton%2C_Watt_and_Murdoch_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1754695.jpg/250px-Boulton%2C_Watt_and_Murdoch_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1754695.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boulton,_Watt_and_Murdoch_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1754695.jpg)

Boulton, Watt and Murdoch, a 1956 statue on Broad Street in Birmingham; the [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") unit of power is the watt, most commonly found as the kW, a replacement for the imperial measurement of [horsepower](/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower").

[Ditherington Flax Mill](/wiki/Ditherington_Flax_Mill "Ditherington Flax Mill") in Shrewsbury was the first [iron-framed building](/wiki/Steel_frame "Steel frame") in the world in 1797. [Thomas Bolton & Sons](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Bolton_%26_Sons&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thomas Bolton & Sons (page does not exist)") of [Froghall](/wiki/Froghall "Froghall"), Staffordshire, made the world's first [transatlantic telegraph cable](/wiki/Transatlantic_telegraph_cable "Transatlantic telegraph cable") in 1857, having supplied a [submarine cable](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable") across the English Channel in 1850. On 10 July 1890, a trunk circuit telephone line was opened between London and Birmingham by the [National Telephone Company](/wiki/National_Telephone_Company "National Telephone Company"); for the first time this allowed phone calls between the London and the north.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The world's first [coaxial cable](/wiki/Coaxial_cable "Coaxial cable") was laid between London and Birmingham in 1936 to give 40 channels for telephone traffic.[[9]](#cite_note-9) and brought into use in 1938, later extended to Manchester in 1940.

[Alexander Parkes](/wiki/Alexander_Parkes "Alexander Parkes") invented the first man-made plastic ([thermoplastic](/wiki/Thermoplastic "Thermoplastic")) in Birmingham in 1856. Princess Square, Wolverhampton, was the site of Britain's first traffic lights in 1927. Infrared cameras were developed at the Royal Radar Establishment in Malvern (with EMI Electronics) in 1967. The world's first [Maglev](/wiki/Maglev "Maglev") train operated at [Birmingham Airport](/wiki/AirRail_Link "AirRail Link") in 1983. The tallest freestanding structure in the region was the chimney of [Ironbridge power station](/wiki/Ironbridge_power_stations "Ironbridge power stations") at 673 ft. [John Baskerville](/wiki/John_Baskerville "John Baskerville") of Birmingham, a former stone carver, largely invented fonts, or [typefaces](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface"), for printing.

See also: [Automotive industry in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_the_United_Kingdom "Automotive industry in the United Kingdom")

Much of the UK's car industry would be centred in Coventry and Birmingham; most of this has now gone. [Midland Motor Cylinder](/w/index.php?title=Midland_Motor_Cylinder&action=edit&redlink=1 "Midland Motor Cylinder (page does not exist)") (part of [Birmid Industries](/w/index.php?title=Birmid_Industries&action=edit&redlink=1 "Birmid Industries (page does not exist)")) of Smethwick was the largest producer of automobile [cylinder blocks](/wiki/Cylinder_block "Cylinder block") in Europe. [Fort Dunlop](/wiki/Fort_Dunlop "Fort Dunlop") was Europe's largest tyre plant. [Metro-Cammell](/wiki/Metro-Cammell "Metro-Cammell") in Birmingham made most of the 1970s and 1980s [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground") trains. [MG Rover](/wiki/MG_Rover_Group "MG Rover Group") (a company of Rover) closed in 2005 (from 1885), The [Ryton plant](/wiki/Ryton_plant "Ryton plant"), which made the [Peugeot 206](/wiki/Peugeot_206 "Peugeot 206"), closed at the end of 2006, with production moving to [Trnava](/wiki/Trnava "Trnava") in Slovakia, and some to a plant at [Kolín](/wiki/Kol%C3%ADn "Kolín") in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). [Alfred Herbert](/wiki/Alfred_Herbert_(company) "Alfred Herbert (company)") of Coventry was the largest [machine-tool manufacturer](/w/index.php?title=Tool_builder&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tool builder (page does not exist)") in the UK for many decades; it was brought down in the 1970s by advancing technology overseas, and complacent strategic decisions of the management, finally closing in 1982; many Midlands manufacturing companies followed similar fates in the 1970s and 1980s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Grazebrook_Beam_Engine.jpg/250px-Grazebrook_Beam_Engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grazebrook_Beam_Engine.jpg)

1817 Boulton & Watt beam blowing engine re-erected on the [Dartmouth Circus](/wiki/Dartmouth_Circus "Dartmouth Circus") roundabout, on the [A4540 Middleway](/wiki/A4540_Middleway "A4540 Middleway") and the A38(M)

[Henry Wiggin & Co](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Wiggin_%26_Co&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henry Wiggin & Co (page does not exist)") of Hereford developed the metal [alloys](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") necessary for other Midlands' (and beyond) automotive and aerospace companies – [Inconel](/wiki/Inconel "Inconel"), [Incoloy](/wiki/Incoloy "Incoloy") and [Nimonic](/wiki/Nimonic "Nimonic"). It was the lack of [vanadium](/wiki/Vanadium "Vanadium") for [high-melting point alloys](/wiki/High-temperature_corrosion "High-temperature corrosion"), caused by Royal Navy action, that prevented German [Me 262](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Me_262 "Messerschmitt Me 262") engines being serviceable; had German Second World War engineers had a greater supply of vanadium and [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum"), the engine life (around 12 hours maximum, from entering service in April 1944 to the end of the war) of their [jet engine](/wiki/Junkers_Jumo_004 "Junkers Jumo 004") would have increased much more, which would have been significant to the war's outcome. [Bristol Siddeley](/wiki/Bristol_Siddeley "Bristol Siddeley") developed the rocket engines for [Black Arrow](/wiki/Black_Arrow "Black Arrow") at Ansty; in fact all of R-R's rocket engines were developed and built there at R-R's Industrial and Marine Gas Turbine Division; Britain's smaller rocket engines for missiles were built by [Bristol Aerojet](/wiki/Bristol_Aerojet "Bristol Aerojet") in what is now [North Somerset](/wiki/North_Somerset "North Somerset"). High Duty Alloys at Redditch constructed (forged) the compressor and turbine blades for Whittle's first engines, and many of the early jet engines; it made [Concorde](/wiki/Concorde "Concorde")'s airframe from the [Hiduminium](/wiki/Hiduminium "Hiduminium") R.R.58 [aluminium alloy](/wiki/Aluminium_alloy "Aluminium alloy").

[Maxaret](/wiki/Maxaret "Maxaret"), the world's first [ABS](/wiki/Anti-lock_braking_system "Anti-lock braking system") [braking](/wiki/Brake "Brake") system, was invented in Coventry by Dunlop in the early 1950s for aircraft; [John Boyd Dunlop](/wiki/John_Boyd_Dunlop "John Boyd Dunlop") was a Scottish vet who had first produced the first pneumatic [tyres](/wiki/Tire "Tire") in 1889. [Matthew Piers Watt Boulton](/wiki/Matthew_Piers_Watt_Boulton "Matthew Piers Watt Boulton"), grandson of Matthew Boulton, and born in the area, invented the [aileron](/wiki/Aileron "Aileron"), an important [flight control surface](/wiki/Flight_control_surfaces "Flight control surfaces") in 1868, decades before the first actual flight. [Triumph Engineering](/wiki/Triumph_Engineering "Triumph Engineering") was a famous motorbike firm in Meriden. About a quarter of all British WWI planes were built in Coventry. The [Jensen Interceptor](/wiki/Jensen_Interceptor "Jensen Interceptor") FF was the first production four-wheel-drive car in the world, designed by Major [Tony Rolt](/wiki/Tony_Rolt "Tony Rolt"), and built at their factory in West Bromwich.

Cadbury launched *[Dairy Milk](/wiki/Cadbury_Dairy_Milk "Cadbury Dairy Milk")* in 1905, *[Bournville](/wiki/Bournville_(chocolate_bar) "Bournville (chocolate bar)")* in 1906, *[Fruit & Nut](/wiki/Cadbury_Dairy_Milk_Fruit_%26_Nut "Cadbury Dairy Milk Fruit & Nut")* in 1928, *Whole Nut* in 1930, *[Cadbury Roses](/wiki/Cadbury_Roses "Cadbury Roses")* in 1938, and the *[Cadbury Creme Egg](/wiki/Cadbury_Creme_Egg "Cadbury Creme Egg")* in 1971. [George](/wiki/George_Cadbury "George Cadbury") and [Richard Cadbury](/wiki/Richard_Cadbury "Richard Cadbury") built their factory in 1879 and [Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville") in 1893, named after the [Bourn brook](/wiki/Bournbrook "Bournbrook"). [Iceland (supermarket)](/wiki/Iceland_(supermarket) "Iceland (supermarket)") opened its first store in Oswestry in 1970 – heralding the onset of frozen food in the UK. [Alfred Bird](/wiki/Alfred_Bird "Alfred Bird") invented egg-free custard in 1837 in Birmingham – accidentally given to guests at his home, being created as his wife had an allergy to eggs; he then invented [baking powder](/wiki/Baking_powder "Baking powder") in 1843 as his wife also had an allergy to [yeast](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast").

### Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Culture")]

[J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien") grew up in Birmingham, Kings Heath, then part of Worcestershire, and was inspired by [Moseley Bog](/wiki/Moseley_Bog "Moseley Bog") and [Sarehole](/wiki/Sarehole "Sarehole"), and perhaps by the [Perrott's Folly](/wiki/Perrott%27s_Folly "Perrott's Folly"). [Philip Larkin](/wiki/Philip_Larkin "Philip Larkin") came from Coventry. [Rowland Hill](/wiki/Rowland_Hill "Rowland Hill") (stamps) was from Kidderminster. The writer [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot") came from [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton"). [Anthony E. Pratt](/wiki/Anthony_E._Pratt "Anthony E. Pratt") from Birmingham invented [Cluedo](/wiki/Cluedo "Cluedo").

[Frederick Gibberd](/wiki/Frederick_Gibberd "Frederick Gibberd") of Coventry designed [Liverpool Metropolitan Cathedral](/wiki/Liverpool_Metropolitan_Cathedral "Liverpool Metropolitan Cathedral"). [Edward Cave](/wiki/Edward_Cave "Edward Cave") from Rugby made Britain's first magazine in 1731 – [The Gentleman's Magazine](/wiki/The_Gentleman%27s_Magazine "The Gentleman's Magazine"). [Philip Astley](/wiki/Philip_Astley "Philip Astley") from Newcastle-under-Lyme invented the modern day circus in 1768 – [Astley's Amphitheatre](/wiki/Astley%27s_Amphitheatre "Astley's Amphitheatre").

The [Castlemorton Common Festival](/wiki/Castlemorton_Common_Festival "Castlemorton Common Festival") in May 1992 near Malvern, led to the [Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994](/wiki/Criminal_Justice_and_Public_Order_Act_1994 "Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994").

The [Nowka Bais](/wiki/Nowka_Bais "Nowka Bais") is a [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_culture "Bengali culture") [boat racing](/wiki/Boat_racing "Boat racing") festival which takes place annually in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"). It is a cultural event in the West Midlands, [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") attracting not only the [Bangladeshi diaspora](/wiki/Bangladeshi_diaspora "Bangladeshi diaspora") but a variety of cultures.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is also the largest kind of [boat race](/wiki/Boat_race "Boat race") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Teenage pregnancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Teenage pregnancy")]

For top-tier authorities in the West Midlands, Stoke-on-Trent has the highest [teenage pregnancy](/wiki/Teenage_pregnancy_and_sexual_health_in_the_United_Kingdom "Teenage pregnancy and sexual health in the United Kingdom") rate. For council districts, [Nuneaton and Bedworth](/wiki/Nuneaton_and_Bedworth "Nuneaton and Bedworth") in Warwickshire has the highest rate closely followed by [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire"). For top-tier authorities, Shropshire has the lowest rate, and for council districts [Malvern Hills](/wiki/Malvern_Hills_(district) "Malvern Hills (district)") has the lowest rate.

### Social deprivation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Social deprivation")]

The region, from studies of multiple deprivation, shows similarities with [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber"), and is more deprived than the neighbouring East Midlands. From the [Indices of deprivation 2007](/wiki/Indices_of_deprivation_2007 "Indices of deprivation 2007"), it can be seen that, in common with [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), the region has more [Lower Area Super Output Areas](/wiki/Super_Output_Areas "Super Output Areas") in the 20% most deprived districts than in the 20% least deprived districts.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The region's most deprived council districts, in descending order, are Birmingham (10th highest in England), Sandwell (14th), Stoke-on-Trent (16th), Wolverhampton (28th), Walsall (45th), Coventry (61st), and Dudley (100th).[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The least deprived districts in 2007 (before Shropshire became a unitary authority in 2009) were Bromsgrove, South Staffordshire, Warwick, Wychavon, and Lichfield. At county level, the least deprived areas, in descending order, were Warwickshire, Worcestershire, Solihull, Staffordshire, and Shropshire.

In March 2011 the region had the second highest overall [unemployment claimant](/wiki/Jobseeker%27s_Allowance "Jobseeker's Allowance") count in England at 4.7%, second to [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"). The highest in the region was Wolverhampton at 7.7%, the joint second highest (with Manchester) unemployment rate in England. Next is Sandwell with 7.1%, Birmingham with 7.0%, and Walsall with 6.4%. The lowest rate in the region is the district of [Stratford-on-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-on-Avon_(district) "Stratford-on-Avon (district)"), with 1.6% – one of the lowest unemployment rates in England.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Regional assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Regional assembly")]

The official representative body of the region is the [West Midlands Leaders Board](/wiki/West_Midlands_Leaders_Board "West Midlands Leaders Board") which has limited administrative functions such as regional planning and economic development. The board is not an elected body, but is made up of members appointed from local councils across the region and is known as a [quango](/wiki/Quango "Quango"). It is based on *Edward Street* in Birmingham, near the [National Indoor Arena](/wiki/National_Indoor_Arena "National Indoor Arena"). From March 2010, the funding decisions at regional level were taken over by [Advantage West Midlands](/wiki/Advantage_West_Midlands "Advantage West Midlands"), the [Regional Development Agency](/wiki/Regional_Development_Agency "Regional Development Agency").

### Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Elections")]

In the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), the Conservatives gained the largest share of the region by popular vote and took control of the number of seats, with 42% of the region's electorate voting Conservative, 33% Labour, 16% UKIP, 6% Liberal Democrat and 3% Green. The Conservatives gained 2 seats with virtually no swing from Labour to Conservative.[[23]](#cite_note-electoralcalculus2015-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_%28West_Midlands%29.svg/250px-United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_%28West_Midlands%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_(West_Midlands).svg)

General Election results in 2017

In the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), [South Staffordshire](/wiki/South_Staffordshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Staffordshire (UK Parliament constituency)") ([Gavin Williamson](/wiki/Gavin_Williamson "Gavin Williamson")) had the second-highest Conservative vote proportion in the UK – 69.8%. [David Firth](/wiki/David_Firth_(statistician) "David Firth (statistician)"), at the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick"), invented the BBC election [exit poll](/wiki/Exit_poll "Exit poll"). 6 ft 9 [Daniel Kawczynski](/wiki/Daniel_Kawczynski "Daniel Kawczynski"), a Shropshire MP, is the tallest MP ever.

Although having a slightly smaller percentage of the vote than the neighbouring [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands"), the geographic area of the West Midlands is more Conservative, due to Labour's vote now consigned to the urban areas of Birmingham, Coventry and Stoke-on-Trent. The number of seats is more favoured towards Labour than the geographic spread, with 35 Conservative seats and 24 Labour. All of Warwickshire, Staffordshire, Herefordshire, Worcestershire and Shropshire is now Conservative.

### Secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Secondary education")]

Selective schools are in low numbers as follows: Birmingham (8), Walsall (2), Wolverhampton (1), Warwickshire (6), Stoke-on-Trent (1), and Telford and Wrekin (2). The highest proportion per head therefore is Warwickshire (its population is between 550,000 and 600,000 people). The other counties and metropolitan boroughs have none, their public education systems are comprehensive in intake. The grammar and independent schools tend to produce pass-rate examination results among the top twenty ranked regionally. Many pupils compete for entrance examinations to attend such long-established [Grammar Schools](/wiki/Grammar_schools_in_England "Grammar schools in England") and most have significant parent sponsorship. In 2016 two of the top ten such schools nationally were in Warwickshire, where in the [CV37 postal district](/wiki/CV_postcode_area "CV postcode area") prices were 34% higher than the county as a whole.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Around 275,000 secondary schools are in the region, the greatest number after the [South East](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"), Greater London and [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At [GCSE](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE") based on % of entrants' pass rates, the best performing local government area in 2010 was [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull"), closely followed by Warwickshire and Shropshire. Dudley, Herefordshire, Telford and Wrekin, Birmingham and Staffordshire (in descending order) are above the English average, at which rate, is approximately Worcestershire. The area consistently having fewest passes is [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell"), followed by Stoke-on-Trent. Struggling pupils in Wolverhampton and Walsall also attain fewer passes than the English average in most GCSE years, sometimes by a very narrow margin. For metropolitan boroughs, Solihull then Dudley perform best. Dudley is the best metropolitan borough at A-level passes and has a consistent post-2000 history of being better than Solihull.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, schools have been [off-rolling](/wiki/Off-rolling "Off-rolling") pupils.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Pupils likely to perform poorly in examinations are expelled before the examinations to improve the school performance in league tables. Expelled pupils then disproportionately get involved in gangs and in crime. Knife crime in the West Midlands is the highest outside London.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2010, regionally in persistent [truancy](/wiki/Truancy "Truancy") at secondary school, Sandwell had the highest rate at 6.9%; [Bromsgrove](/wiki/Bromsgrove_(district) "Bromsgrove (district)") had the lowest at 2.3%.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

### Tertiary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tertiary education")]

There are thirty-seven FE colleges (FECs).[[35]](#cite_note-35) There are six LSCs for the area (which fund FECs), and the [Learning and Skills Council](/wiki/Learning_and_Skills_Council "Learning and Skills Council") head office is based in [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"). The five largest FE colleges in the region – [Bournville College](/wiki/Bournville_College "Bournville College"), [North Warwickshire and Hinckley College](/wiki/North_Warwickshire_and_Hinckley_College "North Warwickshire and Hinckley College"), [Solihull College](/wiki/Solihull_College "Solihull College"), [South & City College Birmingham](/wiki/South_%26_City_College_Birmingham "South & City College Birmingham") and [Stoke-on-Trent College](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent_College "Stoke-on-Trent College") – each have more than 25,000 students.

School children in Shropshire and Solihull are most likely to attend university, followed by Herefordshire, Worcestershire and Warwickshire.

The University of Birmingham is the main university in the region[[37]](#cite_note-37) and has the most funding. It has a large research grant, as does the University of Warwick, which is the next largest in terms of funding. Birmingham and Warwick are members of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") of public research universities. Keele and Aston have a moderate research grant, but none of the other universities do. Keele, although having the largest campus in the UK (by area), is one of the smallest universities in the region. There are medical schools at [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick_Medical_School "Warwick Medical School"), [Keele](/wiki/Keele_University_Medical_School "Keele University Medical School") and [Birmingham](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham_Medical_School "University of Birmingham Medical School"). Birmingham and Warwick receive more than twice as much total income than any other university in the region – around £400 million each.

Around 45% of students are from the region, and 35% from other parts of the UK, while 20% are from overseas. The region attracts students from [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England") owing to good access via the [M40](/wiki/M40_motorway "M40 motorway") and the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line"), but there is a good mix from other regions too, except the North East (especially) and Yorkshire. Students native to the West Midlands are most likely to study in the region (40%), then the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") (12%), the [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") (11%), and then Yorkshire (9%). Very few go to the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England") or the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"). The region has a net export of university students to other regions.

At time of graduation in 2010 almost 60% of graduates remained in the West Midlands, with 10% going to London, 7% to the South-East, and around 5% to the East Midlands. Very few go to Yorkshire, the North-East, or even (neighbouring) Wales.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Railways")]

Served by many lines in the urban areas such as the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line") and branches. The [Welsh Marches Line](/wiki/Welsh_Marches_Line "Welsh Marches Line") and the [Cotswold Line](/wiki/Cotswold_Line "Cotswold Line") transect the region as well as the [Cross Country Route](/wiki/Cross_Country_Route "Cross Country Route") and [Chiltern Line](/wiki/Chiltern_Line "Chiltern Line"). There are plans[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] to reopen the [Gloucestershire Warwickshire Railway](/wiki/Gloucestershire_Warwickshire_Railway "Gloucestershire Warwickshire Railway").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [HS2 (High Speed Two) project](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2") is planned to connect London to Birmingham by 2026.

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Roads")]

Several notable roads pass through the region, with most converging around the central conurbation. The [M5](/wiki/M5_motorway "M5 motorway") motorway, which connects [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England") to the region, passes through [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire"), and through the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_county "West Midlands county"), past [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich"), with its northern terminus at its junction with the [M6](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway") outside [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"). The M6, which has its southern terminus just outside the southeast of the region at its junction with the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") motorway, and which connects the region to [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England"), passes [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire") and [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton") in Warwickshire, [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), and [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford "Stafford") and [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") in Staffordshire. The [M6 toll](/wiki/M6_toll "M6 toll") provides an alternative route to the M6 between [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire") and [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock "Cannock"), passing north of [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield") and just south of [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield").

The [M45](/wiki/M45_motorway "M45 motorway") motorway connects the region through [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") to the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands"), giving access to the north of the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") motorway and, with its eastern terminus at its junction with the [A45](/wiki/A45_road "A45 road") road near the Warwickshire-Northamptonshire border; it passes close to [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire") and [Dunchurch](/wiki/Dunchurch "Dunchurch"). The M42 connects the M5 at [Bromsgrove](/wiki/Bromsgrove "Bromsgrove"), passing around the south and east of Birmingham, joining the M40 and M6, passing [Fordbridge](/wiki/Fordbridge "Fordbridge"),[Kingshurst](/wiki/Kingshurst "Kingshurst") and [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich"), to [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire"), northeast of Birmingham. The [M50](/wiki/M50_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M50 motorway (Great Britain)") connects the M5 from near [Tewkesbury](/wiki/Tewkesbury "Tewkesbury") to [Ross-on-Wye](/wiki/Ross-on-Wye "Ross-on-Wye") in the southwest. The [M54](/wiki/M54_motorway "M54 motorway") connects [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington,_Shropshire "Wellington, Shropshire") in the west, passing [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford"), to the M6 near [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock "Cannock"). The [A5 road](/wiki/A5_road_(Great_Britain) "A5 road (Great Britain)") traverses the region northwest–southeast, passing through [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury"), [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford"), [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock "Cannock"), [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire") and [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton"), also giving access to [Hinckley](/wiki/Hinckley "Hinckley").

The [A40](/wiki/A40_road_(Great_Britain) "A40 road (Great Britain)"), the longest A- road in [Zone 4](/wiki/Great_Britain_road_numbering_scheme#Zoning_system "Great Britain road numbering scheme"), passes through [Ross-on-Wye](/wiki/Ross-on-Wye "Ross-on-Wye") alongside the [A449](/wiki/A449 "A449"), which goes to [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford "Stafford") and [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), and [M50](/wiki/M50_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M50 motorway (Great Britain)") motorway.

The longest elevated road viaduct in the UK is the 3 miles (4,779 m) section from Gravelly Hill to Castle Bromwich on the M6, opened on 24 May 1972; the 3.5 miles (5.6 km) [Bromford Viaduct](/wiki/Bromford_Viaduct "Bromford Viaduct") is the longest viaduct in the UK. The section of the A45 in Coventry from [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall,_Coventry "Willenhall, Coventry") to Allesley in 1939 was one of the UK's first ever large planned road schemes; road schemes on that scale had not been previously built, with few large road schemes outside of London, or were piecemeal.

Princes Square in Wolverhampton had Britain's first automatic traffic lights on 5 November 1927.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] On 13 January 2012, 34-year-old Ben Westwood of Wednesfield, was caught by the police, when speeding at 180 mph, in an [Audi RS5](/wiki/Audi_A5 "Audi A5") with a [Lamborghini](/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini") engine, from Wolverhampton up to Stafford on the M6, and back again. He was travelling so fast that he was outpacing the [Central Counties Air Operations Unit](/wiki/Central_Counties_Air_Operations_Unit "Central Counties Air Operations Unit") [Eurocopter](/wiki/Eurocopter_EC135 "Eurocopter EC135") helicopter. He and the vehicle had been in fifteen [smash and grab](/wiki/Smash_and_grab "Smash and grab") raids and he was jailed for nine years at [Wolverhampton Crown Court](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Crown_Court "Wolverhampton Crown Court") in August 2012.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Transport policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Transport policy")]

As part of the transport planning system, the Regional Assembly is under statutory requirement to produce a [regional transport strategy](/w/index.php?title=Regional_transport_strategy_(UK)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Regional transport strategy (UK) (page does not exist)") (RTS) to provide long term planning for transport in the region. This involves region-wide transport schemes such as those carried out by [Highways England](/wiki/Highways_England "Highways England") and [Network Rail](/wiki/Network_Rail "Network Rail").[[39]](#cite_note-GoWM1-39)

Within the region, the local transport authorities carry out transport planning through the use of a [local transport plan](/wiki/Local_transport_plan "Local transport plan") (LTP) which outlines their strategies, policies and implementation programme.[[40]](#cite_note-DfT1-40) The most recent LTP is that for the period 2006–11. In the West Midlands region, the following transport authorities have published their LTP online: [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"),[[41]](#cite_note-HLTP-41) [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") U.A.,[[42]](#cite_note-ShLTP-42) [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"),[[43]](#cite_note-StLTP-43) [Telford and Wrekin](/wiki/Telford_and_Wrekin "Telford and Wrekin") U.A.,[[44]](#cite_note-T&WLTP-44) [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"),[[45]](#cite_note-WrLTP-45) [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)")[[46]](#cite_note-WMLTP-46) and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire").[[47]](#cite_note-WcLTP-47) The transport authority of [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") U.A. publishes a joint local transport plan in partnership with Staffordshire County Council to cover the North Staffordshire Major Urban Area, which includes Stoke-on-Trent and the more urban parts of [Newcastle-under-Lyme](/wiki/Newcastle-under-Lyme "Newcastle-under-Lyme") and [Staffordshire Moorlands](/wiki/Staffordshire_Moorlands "Staffordshire Moorlands").[[48]](#cite_note-SoTLTP-48)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Sport")]

See also: [Sport in Birmingham](/wiki/Sport_in_Birmingham "Sport in Birmingham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/St_Georges_Park_Aerial_May_2012.jpg/250px-St_Georges_Park_Aerial_May_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Georges_Park_Aerial_May_2012.jpg)

St George's Park is the training ground of the [England national football team](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team").

The [National Sports Centre](/wiki/National_Sports_Centre_(Sport_England) "National Sports Centre (Sport England)") at [Lilleshall Hall](/wiki/Lilleshall_Hall "Lilleshall Hall") is in [Sheriffhales](/wiki/Sheriffhales "Sheriffhales"), Shropshire, north-east of Telford; it was largely established by the [Central Council of Physical Recreation](/wiki/Sport_and_Recreation_Alliance "Sport and Recreation Alliance") in 1949 as a National Recreation Centre; the south of England had theirs at [Bisham Abbey](/wiki/Bisham_Abbey "Bisham Abbey") on the Thames. [St George's Park National Football Centre](/wiki/St_George%27s_Park_National_Football_Centre "St George's Park National Football Centre") is at [Tatenhill](/wiki/Tatenhill "Tatenhill") near [Byrkley Park](/w/index.php?title=Byrkley_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Byrkley Park (page does not exist)") in [Needwood Forest](/wiki/Needwood_Forest "Needwood Forest"), near former the [RAF Tatenhill](/wiki/RAF_Tatenhill "RAF Tatenhill") off the [A515](/wiki/A515_road "A515 road"), four miles west of Burton upon Trent.

The [Tough Guy Competition](/wiki/Tough_Guy_Competition "Tough Guy Competition"), now a widespread sport competition in the US, began in 1987 on a farm at [Perton](/wiki/Perton "Perton") in Staffordshire. The main British athletics championships are held in Birmingham in late June. The [Olympic Movement](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") started at [Much Wenlock](/wiki/Much_Wenlock "Much Wenlock"), and also to the east of region, where [Baron de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin") formulated his ideas for sport and the Olympics at [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") in 1883, with the headmaster [Thomas Arnold](/wiki/Thomas_Arnold "Thomas Arnold"), whose son would be the famous poet [Matthew Arnold](/wiki/Matthew_Arnold "Matthew Arnold") and whose great-grandson would be [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley").

In [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [Midlands Hurricanes](/wiki/Midlands_Hurricanes "Midlands Hurricanes") play in the third tier [League 1](/wiki/RFL_League_1 "RFL League 1").

### Tennis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(region)&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Tennis")]

See also: [History of tennis](/wiki/History_of_tennis "History of tennis")

Britain's first tennis club was founded in 1872 in [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Royal_Leamington_Spa "Royal Leamington Spa"). The modern rules of lawn tennis were developed in 1874 by Leamington Tennis Club. Tennis was pioneered in Edgbaston in 1859, and [Edgbaston Archery and Lawn Tennis Society](/wiki/Edgbaston_Archery_and_Lawn_Tennis_Society "Edgbaston Archery and Lawn Tennis Society") also claims to be the oldest tennis club in the world, where tennis was invented by Major [Harry Gem](/wiki/Harry_Gem "Harry Gem") and the Spaniard [Augurio Perera](/wiki/Augurio_Perera "Augurio Perera").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
